summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Joel Spolsky's 'The Best Software Writing I' anthology, using it as a launching point to discuss the power of storytelling in technical writing. He highlights Joel's central thesis that the 'show, don't tell' principle separates engaging writing from forgettable prose. Through Joel's extended anecdote about an Army Sergeant Major teaching by example, Atwood demonstrates how concrete stories are far more memorable and persuasive than abstract principles. While recommending the book as a valuable survey of excellent blog writing, Atwood also offers his characteristically blunt criticism of a few entries he found lacking.
key_points: The 'show, don't tell' principle is what separates compelling technical writing from boring, unconvincing prose
A concrete story is worth far more than an abstract principle—readers would rather hear ten 400-word stories than one lecture about leadership
Most people are unaware of the excellent software writing that exists in blog form
Even curated 'best of' collections contain entries that miss the mark—quality is subjective
Leading by example (as in the Sergeant Major story) demonstrates principles more effectively than stating them
money_quotes: The author had violated the number one rule of good writing, the 'Show, don't tell' rule. There was not a single story in the book.
I'll bet you'd rather sit through ten of those 400 word stories than have to listen to someone drone on about how 'a good team leader provides inspiration by setting a positive example.'
If he can clean a toilet, I can clean a toilet. There's nothing wrong with cleaning toilets.
The book works quite well as a survey overview of the outstanding blog content that most people still don't even know is out there.
It was chock full of sentences like 'A good team leader provides inspiration by setting a positive example.' What the eff?
key_insight: Concrete stories and examples are infinitely more persuasive and memorable than abstract principles—this is the fundamental rule that separates great technical writing from forgettable advice.
